turns.content: Imagine I'm building a firmware driver for an embedded chip where there is absolutely no standard library available. In my current language, I'd just wrap errors in a generic Exception class and catch them everywhere. But here I can't even use std::error::Error because the environment doesn't have that crate. How do I handle errors if I can't rely on the standard error trait?

You are right to notice that in a no_std context you cannot use std::error::Error since it requires the standard library which isn't present. Instead, you should define your own error types using enums or structs and implement core::fmt::Display for them so they can be printed with fmt::write. This allows you to format errors without needing any external crates beyond core.
That sounds promising, but what if I need multiple error conditions? Can't I just use a Result<T, E> where E is my custom enum?

Yes, you can absolutely use Result<T, E> with your own enum as the error type. This approach gives you compile-time safety without needing dynamic dispatch or trait objects like Box<dyn Error>. You define an enum like ErrorType that lists all possible failure cases, then return Result<YourData, ErrorType> throughout your code.
Wait, so I never need to write any traits or trait objects? That seems too simple. What about when I need something more complex like logging errors with context?

You can still implement sophisticated error handling without using traits by combining your enum-based errors with core::fmt::Display and custom formatting logic in functions. For example, you could write a helper function that takes an ErrorType variant and formats it into a string or bytes before returning it to the caller.
I'm getting frustrated because I keep thinking about how hard this must be compared to my old language. It feels like I'm reinventing everything just to avoid using std::error::Error.

It can feel that way initially, but the real power comes from realizing you don't need dynamic dispatch at all. Every error variant is known at compile time, so there's no runtime cost or memory overhead for boxing or trait objects. You're trading a bit of flexibility in error representation for performance and predictability.
Okay, I understand the performance benefit now. But what about embedded systems where debugging is critical? How do I get more detailed logs without std::panic or println!

For embedded systems you should consider using defmt instead of core::fmt::Display for logging. Defmt provides a lightweight formatting system designed specifically for microcontrollers, allowing you to send structured error messages over UART or I2C without needing the standard library's heavy formatting overhead.
Defmt sounds interesting, but what if my code needs both embedded logging and some richer error reporting when compiled with std enabled? How do I handle that?

You can gate your error handling logic on feature = 'std' using cfg_if or build.rs flags. When the standard library is available, you fall back to std::error::Error and Box<dyn Error>, but when it's not, you use your enum-based errors with core::fmt::Display and defmt for logging.
So I need two different error strategies depending on the environment? That seems complicated to manage across my codebase.

It does require some careful design, but you can centralize your error handling in a single module that conditionally re-exports or adapts based on the feature flags. This way your application code stays clean while the infrastructure handles the differences between no_std and std environments.
I think I finally see how this connects to what I know from my previous experience with dynamic dispatch errors. The trade-off is clear now, even if it took some frustration to get there.

Exactly, and that realization will make your no_std code much more robust. You now have a solid foundation for handling errors in environments without the standard library using enums, core::fmt::Display, defmt, and conditional compilation.
